source.verbatim_text: This document defines the requirements for contractual data to be delivered by the contractor to the Government. The data requirements are set forth in each Data Requirements Description (DRD) and shall govern that data required by the contract. The contractor shall furnish data defined by the DRDs as listed on the Contract Data Requirements List (CDRL) by category of data, attached hereto, and made a part of this contract. Such data shall be prepared, deliver, and maintain to NASA in accordance with the requirements set forth within this document.

atomic_text: The data requirements are set forth in each Data Requirements Description (DRD) and shall govern that data required by the contract.

source.verbatim_text: This document sets forth the data requirements in each DRD and shall govern the data required under this contract. The Contractor shall furnish data defined by the DRDs listed by category of data. Such data shall be prepared, maintained, and delivered to National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA) in accordance with the requirements set forth herein. NASA-Owned/Contractor-Held records shall be managed by the Contractor in accordance with Title 36 of the Code of Federal Regulations, Chapter XII B. Records Management and NPD 1440.6, NASA Records Management. The records shall be organized in accordance with the instructions in NPR.1441.1, NASA Records Management Program Requirements as applicable. The Contractor shall disposition records and non-records in accordance with NPR 1441.1, which has been approved by NASA. All questions on records management issues shall be directed through the Contracting Officer or the Contracting Officer Representative.

source.verbatim_text: 1 That information and documentation which requires NASA approval prior to release. Approved Type 1 information and documentation shall be controlled, and deviations from or changes to the concepts, techniques, and/or requirements stated therein shall require NASA approval prior to implementation. All work under this contract covered by Type 1 documents shall be performed in accordance with those approved documents. The Contracting Officer's Representative has approval authority and signs the data prior to its release.

source.verbatim_text: 2 That information and documentation for which NASA reserves a time-limited right to disapprove. Type 2 data shall be submitted to designated repository for review not less than 45 calendar days prior to its release for use or implementation. The Contractor shall clearly identify the target release date in the 'submitted for review' transmittal. If the Contractor has not received any comment prior to the target released date, the document may be released for appropriate use. Any NASA comment received shall be appropriately dispositioned and resubmitted prior to implementation, restarting the 45-day review period.

source.verbatim_text: Existing Contractor internal documents may be used to meet the data requirements of the DRD to the extent practicable. The DRDs will call out any special format requirements if they are required. The Contractor's submittal in response to the DRDs shall be provided in an electronic format that meets four basic requirements: 'Readable,' 'Printable,' 'Searchable,' and 'Downloadable' by NASA utilizing publicly available off-the-shelf software. If the electronic format is not supported by publicly available off-the-shelf software, the Contractor shall provide NASA with the necessary software and approach to support the four basic requirements

source.section: 3.4 DATA RESTRICTION DETERMINATION AND MARKING REQUIREMENTS
source.verbatim_text: The contractor shall properly mark data in accordance with the data rights clause(s) included in the contract. The contractor shall mark data deliverable sections with appropriate data restrictions and export classifications. The contractor shall not apply the most restrictive classification to the entire data delivery if only applicable to one section. If NASA does not agree with the contractor applied data restriction, the CO shall request supporting rationale for applied data restrictions. If NASA does not agree with the rationale, the CO shall return the data to the contractor, cancel the markings, or ignore the markings consistent with the procedures set forth in the "data rights" clause(s) contained in the contract and per U.S. export laws.

atomic_text: The contractor shall properly mark data in accordance with the data rights clause(s) included in the contract.

source.section: 5.0 REFERENCE TO OTHER DOCUMENTS AND DATA DELIVERABLES IN DATA SUBMITTALS
source.verbatim_text: The Contractor's submittal of a DRD deliverable may refer to other documents and/or other DRD deliverables. At the time of submission of the DRD deliverable, any referenced document within the DRD deliverable shall be made available to NASA. The Contractor shall provide NASA with access to any referenced document and the location of that data within the referenced document. Any reference made to data associated with another DRD deliverable that is required separately by the contract, shall include the DRD number of and location of the data within the referenced DRD deliverable.

atomic_text: At the time of submission of the DRD deliverable, any referenced document within the DRD deliverable shall be made available to NASA.

source.verbatim_text: a. The IMS is an integrated, logically driven, network-based schedule that is vertically and horizontally traceable. Tasks should be at a level of detail to allow for discrete progress measurement and shall include visibility into the overall development flow which typically includes design, fabrication, assembly, integration, test, and delivery phase of each end item delivery. (Note -Task/milestone titles should be clear, concise, and reflect the scope and output (i.e., deliverable) so that the task content can be understood as standalone data without its associated summary level task also being shown.) Expectation for schedule quality is to follow GAO schedule best practices and schedule health checks.

atomic_text: Tasks should be at a level of detail to allow for discrete progress measurement and shall include visibility into the overall development flow which typically includes design, fabrication, assembly, integration, test, and delivery phase of each end item delivery. (Note -Task/milestone titles should be clear, concise, and reflect the scope and output (i.e., deliverable) so that the task content can be understood as standalone data without its associated summary level task also being shown.) Expectation for schedule quality is to follow GAO schedule best practices and schedule health checks.

source.verbatim_text: b. Tasks and milestones from subcontractors and the commercial launch vehicle providers shall be included within the prime contractor's IMS with sufficient detail to develop a realistic critical path. Their data shall be consistent, statused monthly, and based on the same cutoff date to enable a realistic critical path.

atomic_text: b. Tasks and milestones from subcontractors and the commercial launch vehicle providers shall be included within the prime contractor's IMS with sufficient detail to develop a realistic critical path.

source.verbatim_text: c. The IMS shall have traceability to a Contractor specified Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) (or equivalent), subcontractor activity, and the Performance Work Statement. This traceability should be accomplished by using data fields provided within the scheduling tool.

atomic_text: c. The IMS shall have traceability to a Contractor specified Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) (or equivalent), subcontractor activity, and the Performance Work Statement.

source.verbatim_text: d. Custom/User-Defined Fields: Contractor shall reserve 7 schedule fields for the purpose of helping the Government navigate, analyze, and health-check the IMS. Fields shall include Schedule Margin, Lifecycle Review, Payment Milestones, Government Task Agreements (GTA)/Government Furnished Equipment (GFE), Critical Path, Mission Critical items (as defined by NASA), and Major Supplier Activity.

atomic_text: d. Custom/User-Defined Fields: Contractor shall reserve 7 schedule fields for the purpose of helping the Government navigate, analyze, and health-check the IMS.

source.verbatim_text: e. The IMS shall clearly identify tasks or activities which have a dependency on a NASA delivery or approval (for example, delivery of a GFE), and the availability of a NASA facility.

source.verbatim_text: f. If schedule margin is included in the IMS, it shall be as a defined task and clearly labeled as 'Schedule Margin' or 'Contingency'. (Note - Schedule margin is a determined amount of time that has been added to the schedule by the management team and is to be used and controlled by the project manager to buffer against future impacts that are impossible to predict.)

source.verbatim_text: g. The IMS should contain Level of Effort (LOE) identification. If LOE tasks (i.e., management, miscellaneous support, monitoring, etc.) are included within the IMS they should be clearly labeled as such. If LOE tasks are included, they shall be added in a manner that does not impact discrete tasks/milestones.

source.verbatim_text: h. Critical Path: The schedule tool shall automatically calculate the longest path in the Native Schedule File. If multiple critical paths are identified, the primary, secondary, and tertiary critical paths should be identified using a custom field for Critical Path.

atomic_text: h. Critical Path: The schedule tool shall automatically calculate the longest path in the Native Schedule File.

source.verbatim_text: i. Constraints: The IMS should use minimal constraints. However, if constraints are used, the IMS shall identify the constraints (including any deadlines) applied to tasks. Provide justification for all constraints external to the Native Schedule file.

source.verbatim_text: Scope: All contracts that purchase, lease, network to, or otherwise utilize Government- funded IT (as defined by the Clinger-Cohen Act of 1996 and referenced by Office of Management and Budget (OMB) Circular A-130 must comply with NASA IT Security Requirements.

atomic_text: Scope: All contracts that purchase, lease, network to, or otherwise utilize Government- funded IT (as defined by the Clinger-Cohen Act of 1996 and referenced by Office of Management and Budget (OMB) Circular A-130 must comply with NASA IT Security Requirements.

source.verbatim_text: The Federal Information Security Modernization Act (FISMA) and Executive Branch policy require external providers that process, store, or transmit federal information or operate information systems on behalf of the federal government to meet the same security and privacy requirements as federal agencies.

atomic_text: The Federal Information Security Modernization Act (FISMA) and Executive Branch policy require external providers that process, store, or transmit federal information or operate information systems on behalf of the federal government to meet the same security and privacy requirements as federal agencies.

source.verbatim_text: The Contractor shall submit an ITSMP for its unclassified technology information resources. This plan shall describe the policy, processes, and procedures that will be followed to ensure appropriate security of Information Technology (IT) resources that are developed, processed, or used under this contract. The Contractor's ITSMP shall be compliant with the IT security requirements in accordance with Federal and NASA policies as referenced in OMB Circular A-130, Managing Information as a Strategic Resource and NPR 2810.1(series), NASA Information Security Policy.

atomic_text: The Contractor shall submit an ITSMP for its unclassified technology information resources.

source.verbatim_text: b. Addressing all applicable information security requirements, including vulnerability scanning and mitigation, maintaining secure operating system configuration, patch/configuration management, contingency planning, and protection of sensitive data in transit and at rest.

source.verbatim_text: c. Information security, privacy, incident management and responses, including coordination with NASA Security Operations Center (SOC), Center Chief Information Security Officer (CISO), and Center Privacy Manager as required. The contractor shall immediately notify the NASA SOC, CO, and COR once an incident has been identified.

source.verbatim_text: d. Users are knowledgeable of NASA information security policies and procedures when handling NASA data.

The ITSP shall be kept up to date as changes to the baseline configuration of the system occur and shall be documented in the IT Security Plan.

source.verbatim_text: a. Contractor employees subject to this contract, defined as those requiring physical access to NASA facilities or electronic access to NASA systems, shall complete the NASA approved IT Security Awareness Training annually. NASA Cybersecurity and Privacy Awareness training is available through the SATERN online system and must be completed prior to access to NASA information and systems. Completion of the training is tracked automatedly by NASA and must recur each year by the anniversary of the initial training as a condition of continued access. Cybersecurity and Privacy training is updated throughout the year as needed, so there is no defined training period.

source.verbatim_text: Scope: The CM Plan defines the requirements, responsibilities, and processes and procedures for the Contractor's CM system pursuant to SAE-EIA-649-2, Configuration Management Acquisition Requirements for Aeronautics and Space and AS9100, Quality Management System Requirements for Aviation, Space and Defense Organizations. The CM plan shall describe in re lation to the elements below to the Contractor's CM organization, policies, procedures, implementation approach, and control systems that are to be used to ensure proper performance of CM activities. The CM plan shall also describe the configuration management activities, policies, and systems to be employed to support the NASA change evaluation and control process for the approval of program requirements as well as for the submission of Variances.

source.verbatim_text: Scope: The reports outlined below shall be submitted at the frequency and format specified. Fiscal year is defined as the period from October 1 through September 30. Reports shall not combine more than one fiscal year. If the contract is for only a portion of the fiscal year, the reports shall be submitted for that portion of the fiscal year. When a contract ends or is terminated prior to the end of a fiscal year, the Contractor shall provide reports for the activities performed during the period of performance that the contract was in effect and the final reports shall be submitted no later than 30 days of the contract end date. If upon concurrence from the JSC Environmental Management Office ('EMO') Chief and approval from the CO/COR, any report described herein is deemed not applicable and/or there is no data to report, the report to JSC EMO shall have a statement to that effect, including the Contractor certification that reporting will be initiated/resumed if the Contractor's (or subcontractor's) activities trigger any of the required reports. Because the Center Director compiles data and certifies environmental compliance reports, the Contractor shall include a certification statement within each required report, indicating that all the information provided is true, accurate and complete, and that the Contractor's representative is aware that there are civil and criminal penalties for submitting false information, including immediate contract termination

source.verbatim_text: 2. Industrial and Hazardous Waste Minimization, Pollution Prevention, and Recycling The Contractor shall implement and accurately document efforts to avoid the generation of industrial solid and hazardous waste requiring offsite disposal (e.g. incineration and landfilling). Construction and Demolition (C&D) waste disposal in landfills shall likewise be minimized, and efforts to divert C&D waste from landfills will be documented so as to achieve the JSC prescribed diversion rate goals. See section II.b, for additional requirements applicable to C&D activities or contracts. The Contractor shall actively participate in JSC and NASA sustainability program initiatives, including but not limited to energy and water use reduction, process equipment and supplies excessing through the JSC Redistribution and Utilization (R&U) Office, pollution prevention/waste reduction, composting and recycling programs (e.g. paper, cardboard, scrap metals, wood, plastic, etc).

source.verbatim_text: The Contractor shall track and accurately report to JSC EMO, each November 1, any new process improvements or programs undertaken by the Contractor (or subcontractors) that have contributed to pollution prevention or waste reduction (hazardous and non-hazardous) during the previous fiscal year. Waste reduction means preventing or decreasing the amount of waste that would normally go to the landfill (trash) being generated through waste prevention ***, reuse, recycling ****, or purchasing recycled and environmentally preferable products. The information will be included in JSC's annual report to NASA HQ on waste generation and reduction activities. Limit responses to one page or less per item. The response should include a description of the activity, the materials or wastes generated, reduced, an estimated volume or weight of reduction for each line item, whether onsite recycling or reuse programs were utilized, and a contact name and phone number for a person knowledgeable about the reduction activity. Additional information may be required in accordance with regulatory, Agency or Center-specific requirements. The Contractor shall contact the JSC Environmental Info Line at (281) 483-6207 or send an email to JSC-Environmental-Office@nasa.gov within 15 days of contract award and annually thereafter, to inquire about reports format changes and obtain a copy of any applicable report spreadsheet or report template forms and seek guidance on accurate recordkeeping and reporting.

source.verbatim_text: If the Contractor engages in activities associated with construction, facility modification, or demolition projects, the Contractor shall track and report to JSC EMO the total weight (in pounds) of material generated, sent to incineration or landfill, and materials diverted from landfill/incineration by media (e.g. scrap metal, wood, concrete, soil, building materials, steel, tiles, carpet, glass, furniture, etc), any recycling revenues/proceeds obtained and if those proceeds went to the JSC Recycling funds or not. This report shall also include activities performed by subcontractors working under the Contractor's purview. The Contractor shall note any shipments or waste streams managed by the JSC EMO or any others onsite program to avoid double counting. The report shall not combine more than one fiscal year and is due within 30 days of completion of all waste generating and recycling activities and final waste shipments associated with the project (or project end), and in no case shall the submittal be later than November 1. The Contractor shall include with the report submission to JSC EMO, copies of the waste/materials shipment document (e.g. Bill of Ladings) and recycling receipts (rebates). The Contractor shall use JF 845, Construction and Demolition Recycling Record, or an approved equivalent format, to report this data.

The Contractor shall use JF 845, Construction and Demolition Recycling Record, or an approved equivalent format, to report this data.

source.verbatim_text: If the Contractor engages in activities associated with construction, facility modification, or demolition projects, the Contractor shall track and report to JSC EMO the total weight (in pounds) of material generated, sent to incineration or landfill, and materials diverted from landfill/incineration by media (e.g. scrap metal, wood, concrete, soil, building materials, steel, tiles, carpet, glass, furniture, etc), any recycling revenues/proceeds obtained and if those proceeds went to the JSC Recycling funds or not. This report shall also include activities performed by subcontractors working under the Contractor's purview. The Contractor shall note any shipments or waste streams managed by the JSC EMO or any others onsite program to avoid double counting. source.verbatim_text: If the Contractor, or subcontractors working under the Contractor's purview, engage in waste generation activities (other than Construction and Demolition covered in section II.b) associated with the collection, management, and sale of recyclable materials/equipment and diverts those materials/equipment wastes from landfill or incineration, the Contractor shall track and report the monthly amounts and types of waste/material generated/recycled (or diverted from landfill or incineration), any associated costs and the proceeds from the sale of the recyclable materials managed by the contract. Recyclables materials that the contractor may be managing in the performance of the contract include, but would not be limited to: mixed paper, cardboard, plastic/glass bottles, aluminum cans, organic material diverted to onsite/offsite composting, pallets (wooden/plastic), scrap metal, aluminum, glass and wood products, concrete, soil/dirt, office supplies, electronics, excess property for sales/donations/recycling/reuse or reconditioning, recyclable resins/catalysts sent for reuse or reconditioning, and other recyclable materials/products including authorized industrial solid wastes such lamps, batteries and spent tires. . The report shall be submitted to the JSC EMO by the 15th of each calendar month. Data reported shall include pounds generated and shipped for each type of waste/material, revenue received, and recycling/disposal costs for each type of waste or recyclable material for the previous calendar month. If data is not available at that time, the report shall note the unavailable data and provide it with the next available month's submission, specifying when the amounts are for the previous month.

source.verbatim_text: The Contractor shall ensure that the destination facilities and haulers have all the necessary permits, licenses, or registrations to operate, and have been approved by the JSC EMO prior to use. source.verbatim_text: 5. Associated Contractor Agreements (ACA) During the contract phase-in period and no later than 90 days of contract start, the Contractor shall develop, maintain, and update, as necessary, an Associated Contractor Agreement (ACA) with the EMO Environmental Support Services Contractor (ESSC), specifying the nature of the required reports, frequencies, and expectations for timely, accurate, and complete documentation and overall compliance with JSC applicable environmental compliance requirements. The Contractor shall send notification to the CO, COR and EMO, within 15 days of ACA execution or updates/changes, as applicable, specifying the nature of the ACA requirements and applicable changes made. Within 90 days of contract start, the Contractor shall notify the CO, COR and EMO on the status of the ACA negotiations (unless the ACA is executed prior to this date) or submit data supporting that this requirement should be waived. Note that the Contractor may voluntarily submit to the CO, COR and EMO Office, a copy of the referenced ACA.

source.verbatim_text: b. All plans shall provide information demonstrating the Extent of Commitment to utilize small business concerns and to support their development. source.verbatim_text: Scope: The Government Property Management Plan defines the Contractor's use, maintenance, repair, protection, and preservation of Government personal property. It shall describe the Contractor's approach to receiving, handling, stocking, maintaining, protecting, and issuing Government property (equipment and material). The Plan should include interaction and Department/ Office responsibilities. The Contractor shall submit detailed property procedures, which are separate from the Property Management Plan, within 60 days after the contract start date.

atomic_text: It shall describe the Contractor's approach to receiving, handling, stocking, maintaining, protecting, and issuing Government property (equipment and material).

source.verbatim_text: a. The NF 1018 report provides annual summary-level property management and financial data on Government-furnished and contractor-acquired NASA property.

source.verbatim_text: b. The NF 1018 shall be completed in accordance with NFS Subpart 1845.7101, NFS 1852.245-73, and any supplemental guidance provided by NASA.

source.verbatim_text: c. Contractors shall report all NASA-owned property in US dollars, regardless of location.

source.verbatim_text: d. Negative or zero reports are required.

source.verbatim_text: a. source.verbatim_text: b. Monthly Property Financial Reports are required with item level supporting data. This data shall be submitted for all items with an acquisition cost of $1,000,000 or more, in the contractor's and its subcontractors' possession, in the following classifications: real property, equipment, special test equipment, special tooling, and agency peculiar property (if applicable). Monthly reporting is not required for property in the above classifications with an acquisition cost under $1,000,000. Monthly data shall also be submitted for items of any acquisition cost in the classifications of materials and contract work-in-process (WIP). Itemized monthly data is required for material line items of $1,000,000 and over. Itemized monthly line-item data is required for WIP. Summary monthly data is required for material items under $1,000,000. Other software (not integrated into a piece of Government property that is necessary to operate that item) shall be considered an individual reporting item if the acquisition cost is $1,000,000 or more and expected to have a useful life of 2 years or more.

source.verbatim_text: The Contractor shall prepare a report of all IT expenditures consistent with the Agency ' s guidelines and policies regarding the management and reporting of IT resources, to include the annual CPIC data in support of the agency ' s budget formulation process. Any additional reporting requirements associated with CPIC reporting and data collection process will be covered by this DRD.

source.verbatim_text: In conformance with IT management and reporting processes, to include CPIC, Contractors shall participate in data collection and reporting efforts. Contractors shall furnish the data needed for the Agency to comply with statutory and OMB requirements including but not limited to those documented in OMB Circular A-11. Accurate and complete data submissions are to be made in a manner consistent with the reporting structure, and within the timeframes established for the Center.

source.verbatim_text: 11. Distribution: All deliverables required by this DRD, except for the C-SCRM plan, shall be submitted electronically to the CO, COR, and NASA Organization ' s Information System Security Officer (ISSO) (i.e., Security Officer). Approval will be provided by the ISSO. If the organization does not have an ISSO, approval will be provided by the NASA Information Owner. C-SCRM plans shall be submitted to and approved by the ICT/C-SCRM Service Element Lead at agency-dl-ocio-cys-cp-scrm@mail.nasa.gov.

source.verbatim_text: Contractor employees subject to this contract, defined as those requiring physical access to NASA facilities or electronic access to NASA systems, shall complete the NASA approved IT Security Awareness Training annually. NASA Cybersecurity and Privacy Awareness training is available through the SATERN online system and must be completed prior to access to NASA information and systems. Completion of the training is tracked automatedly by NASA and must recur each year by the anniversary of the initial training as a condition of continued access. Cybersecurity and Privacy training is updated throughout the year as needed, so there is no defined training period.

source.verbatim_text: The Verification, Validation, and Certification Plan (VVCP) shall provide a detail description of the Contractor's verification, validation, and certification methodology and processes to satisfy NASA CLDS requirements per SOW 1.3 CLDS Requirements and Standards, including any verifications and validations to be completed via the On-orbit Demonstration. The VVCP is consist of 3 major components (verification and validation, certification, and on-orbit demonstration) and the separate Annex.

source.verbatim_text: The verification and validation (V&V) component shall provide a detail description of the Contractor's verification and validation approach for implementing the verification and validation program, as well as a detail description of the planned verification and validation requirements and methods. source.verbatim_text: · Statement Of Work (SOW) 2.3 Government Insight

atomic_text: · Statement Of Work (SOW) 2.3 Government Insight

source.verbatim_text: Scope: The Insight Implementation Plan describes the Contractor's plan for providing NASA insight throughout the contract. In addition to the Systems developed by the contractor, insight is required to ensure United States Government (USG) crew safety and NASA's mission success .

atomic_text: In addition to the Systems developed by the contractor, insight is required to ensure United States Government (USG) crew safety and NASA's mission success .

source.verbatim_text: The Insight Implementation Plan shall establish a cooperative environment that ensures an effective working relationship between NASA and the Contractor dedicated to a successful Commercial LEO Destination Contract (CLDC) performance. This includes the approach to accommodating NASA insight of technical and programmatic performance, including safety and mission assurance while still allowing the Contractor to maintain the efficiencies and effectiveness of their normal commercial processes. The Insight Implementation Plan shall address the following:

atomic_text: The Insight Implementation Plan shall establish a cooperative environment that ensures an effective working relationship between NASA and the Contractor dedicated to a successful Commercial LEO Destination Contract (CLDC) performance.

source.verbatim_text: 1. How the Contractor proposes accommodating NASA's timely and open access, including membership on technical and programmatic review boards, into activities and data used for addressing mutual risks and achieving successful NASA approval of deliverables and milestones, Commercial LEO Destination System (CLDS) certification, an endorsement of flight readiness, mission planning, and successful operation of the CLDS.

atomic_text: 1. How the Contractor proposes accommodating NASA's timely and open access, including membership on technical and programmatic review boards, into activities and data used for addressing mutual risks and achieving successful NASA approval of deliverables and milestones, Commercial LEO Destination System (CLDS) certification, an endorsement of flight readiness, mission planning, and successful operation of the CLDS.

source.verbatim_text: 2. How the Contractor proposes accommodating NASA's timely and open access to data, both remotely and on-site, in a useable and readable format; and provide NASA the ability to locate and review all data used in performance of this contract, to include technical data, mission planning data, operational data, Risk Based Assessments (RBA) data, supporting data/information, configuration management/control data, to include analytical models and administrative and management information with the exception of financial information.

atomic_text: 2. How the Contractor proposes accommodating NASA's timely and open access to data, both remotely and on-site, in a useable and readable format; and provide NASA the ability to locate and review all data used in performance of this contract, to include technical data, mission planning data, operational data, Risk Based Assessments (RBA) data, supporting data/information, configuration management/control data, to include analytical models and administrative and management information with the exception of financial information.

source.verbatim_text: 3. How the Contractor proposes accommodating NASA's observation of key development and verification tests both at the system/subsystem level and integrated level (i.e. CLD to Visiting Vehicle)

atomic_text: 3. How the Contractor proposes accommodating NASA's observation of key development and verification tests both at the system/subsystem level and integrated level (i.e. CLD to Visiting Vehicle)

source.verbatim_text: Scope: The IPAITP shall describe the Contractor's approaches to planning and executing testing, analysis, and demonstration of the Commercial Low Earth Orbit Destination System (CLDS) at multiple levels of assembly in order to support overall system verification, validation, demonstration, and certification.

source.verbatim_text: Contents: The Plan shall provide an overview of the Contractor's integrated performance analysis and integrated testing program. source.verbatim_text: The plan shall identify integrated system performance analysis to be conducted for the CLDS lifecycle. The plan shall define how the Contractor analysis will determine appropriate performance margins during nominal and off-nominal scenarios related to crew safety (Pre-launch, launch, transit, landing, post-landing, on-orbit) and account for degradation of vehicles, elements, and systems over their lifetime. NASA recommends at a minimum the following analysis:

atomic_text: The plan shall identify integrated system performance analysis to be conducted for the CLDS lifecycle.

source.verbatim_text: Contents: The Task Analysis is implemented according to the Task Analysis approach in the approved DRD CLDC-108.1 Human Systems Integration Plan. Task Analysis includes all tasks that require human interactions to operate, monitor, or make decisions starting with pre-launch and continuing through flight, landing/docking, and post-flight. Task analysis data fields are defined and approved by NASA in the contractor's HSI Plan and are expected to include: mission phase, task/subtask name, system/subsystem, description of task, task steps, task type (e.g., nominal, contingency, emergency), task criticality, minimum number crew required, parallel or serial task, task dependencies, trigger to start task and trigger to end task, time constraint/time to effect, tool required, worksite, interface element, illumination needed, out-of-view controls, visual task, posture, restrained, constrained or obstructed reach, critical anthropometric dimension and value, critical strength motion and value, restraints and mobility aids needed, gravity environment, vibration environment, shock environment suit condition, crew demand (expected cognitive or physical workload).

atomic_text: Task analysis data fields are defined and approved by NASA in the contractor's HSI Plan and are expected to include: mission phase, task/subtask name, system/subsystem, description of task, task steps, task type (e.g., nominal, contingency, emergency), task criticality, minimum number crew required, parallel or serial task, task dependencies, trigger to start task and trigger to end task, time constraint/time to effect, tool required, worksite, interface element, illumination needed, out-of-view controls, visual task, posture, restrained, constrained or obstructed reach, critical anthropometric dimension and value, critical strength motion and value, restraints and mobility aids needed, gravity environment, vibration environment, shock environment suit condition, crew demand (expected cognitive or physical workload).

source.verbatim_text: i. Regulatory compliance. The Contractor is responsible to ensure compliance with all federal, local, and state laws current at the time governing the development, production, purchase, storage, packaging, and transport of food. The laws and regulations include but are not limited to: Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) Title 21and Title 9; Food and Drug Administration (FDA) Food Safety Modernization Act (FSMA), Current Good Manufacturing Practices (cGMP); Hazard Analysis, and Risk-Based Preventive Controls, etc. Any foods imported from outside of the United States need to comply with the FDA FSMA rule on the Foreign Supplier Verification Program (FSVP) and applicable local, federal, and state government import requirements.

source.verbatim_text: Scope: The Safety and Mission Assurance (SMA) Plan addresses SMA philosophy, organization, approach, and the related processes and activities needed for program safety and mission success for the lifecycle of the contract and for all phases of mission execution. The plan addresses the approach to the major Safety and Mission Assurance components to include (1) Safety and Mission Assurance Management, (2) Hazard Analysis and System Safety, (4) Software Safety and Assurance, (4) Quality Assurance, and (5) Reliability, Maintainability, Logistics, and Supportability (RMLS). The level of detail in the plan directly correlates with the nature and complexity of the effort required to meet program requirements and objectives.

atomic_text: The level of detail in the plan directly correlates with the nature and complexity of the effort required to meet program requirements and objectives.

source.verbatim_text: Scope: The Safety and Mission Assurance (SMA) Plan addresses SMA philosophy, organization, approach, and the related processes and activities needed for program safety and mission success. The plan addresses the approach to the major Safety and Mission Assurance components to include (1) Safety and Mission Assurance Management, (2) Hazard Analysis and System Safety, (4) Software Safety and Assurance, and (4) Quality Assurance. The level of detail in the plan directly correlates with the nature and complexity of the effort required to meet program requirements and objectives.

atomic_text: The level of detail in the plan directly correlates with the nature and complexity of the effort required to meet program requirements and objectives.

source.verbatim_text: Scope: The Safety and Mission Assurance (SMA) Plan addresses SMA philosophy, organization, approach, and the related processes and activities needed for program safety and mission success. The plan addresses the approach to the major Safety and Mission Assurance components to include (1) Safety and Mission Assurance Management, (2) Hazard Analysis and System Safety, (4) Software Safety and Assurance, and (4) Quality Assurance. The level of detail in the plan directly correlates with the nature and complexity of the effort required to meet program requirements and objectives.

atomic_text: The level of detail in the plan directly correlates with the nature and complexity of the effort required to meet program requirements and objectives.

source.verbatim_text: a. The SMA function's organizational structure, roles and responsibilities, and key organizational and safety process interfaces including with management and engineering design teams.

Describes the Contractor's approach (including process, format, content, data rights, and schedule) for providing NASA access to systems and documentation required to implement the hazard analysis and system safety process, including systems and documentation/templates needed to develop safety data packages for NASA payloads.

source.verbatim_text: 3. Software Safety and Assurance Plan: A detailed description of the Contractor's approach to a software safety and assurance program for software development, operation, and maintenance activities that meets the intent of NASA-STD-8739.8B, including documentation of procedures, processes, requirements, metrics, tools, techniques, and methods to be used throughout the lifecycle of the CLDS. The plan addresses the flow down of Software Safety and Assurance requirements to any subcontractor who develops, tests, maintains, operates, or provides services for the software.

source.verbatim_text: iv. Supply Chain Risk Management -Describe the Contractor's approach for ensuring and verifying quality for all types of materials /parts/components the Contractor purchases including the quality management system requirements used for various acquisition types (e.g., systems, parts, materials, special processes); design and construction quality control requirements; the level of control exercised over the suppliers including how suppliers are approved, monitored, and maintained with controls for supplier non-conformances processing; and how externally sourced materials and products are certified as conforming prior to integration into systems. If non-AS9100 certified suppliers are utilized, describe methodology for ensuring equivalent risk through increased requirements flow-down, surveillance, and/or Contractor verification activities.

source.verbatim_text: · SOW Section 2.1 Program Management, Quarterly Review Contents

source.verbatim_text: A risk refers to anything that can prevent the Contractor from meeting contract objectives. All forms of risk shall be managed including safety, technical (hardware and software), programmatic, supportability, cost, and schedule risks. For an undesirable risk or event that has occurred or occurrence that cannot be stopped or directly controlled, the risk will be converted into a 'problem.' Problems will be presented as part of the risk reporting process.

atomic_text: All forms of risk shall be managed including safety, technical (hardware and software), programmatic, supportability, cost, and schedule risks.

source.verbatim_text: 1. Introduction - Specify the program/project risk objectives and policy toward risk. Explain the purpose, scope, assumptions, constraints, key ground rules, and policy pertaining to the project risk management process.

source.verbatim_text: Interrelationship : Statement Of Work (SOW) Section 2.11.5 Orbital Debris, Jettisons, and Deorbit

atomic_text: Interrelationship : Statement Of Work (SOW) Section 2.11.5 Orbital Debris, Jettisons, and Deorbit

source.verbatim_text: Contents: The Jettison Plan describes the Contractor's strategies and methods for the protecting crew, vehicles, and the mitigation of space debris due to jettisoned items consistent with the guidelines and practices of the U.S. Government Orbital Debris Mitigation Standard Practices (November 2019), the InterAgency Space Debris Coordination Committee (IADC) Space Debris Mitigation Guidelines (February 2025), and the Space Debris Mitigation Guidelines of the United Nations Committee on the Peaceful Uses of Outer Space, (2010).

atomic_text: Contents: The Jettison Plan describes the Contractor's strategies and methods for the protecting crew, vehicles, and the mitigation of space debris due to jettisoned items consistent with the guidelines and practices of the U.S. Government Orbital Debris Mitigation Standard Practices (November 2019), the InterAgency Space Debris Coordination Committee (IADC) Space Debris Mitigation Guidelines (February 2025), and the Space Debris Mitigation Guidelines of the United Nations Committee on the Peaceful Uses of Outer Space, (2010).

source.verbatim_text: 1. A policy statement that describes the Contractor's commitment to preserve the near -Earth space environment and to mitigate the risk to human life and space missions due to jettisoned items.

atomic_text: 1. A policy statement that describes the Contractor's commitment to preserve the near -Earth space environment and to mitigate the risk to human life and space missions due to jettisoned items.

source.verbatim_text: 2. A management plan for jettison related activities.

atomic_text: 2. A management plan for jettison related activities.

source.verbatim_text: 3. The jettison candidate identification, analysis, and approval process, including criteria intended to quantify and control additional orbital debris risk from any jettison candidate and the risk of collision a jettison candidate poses to the Destination and Visiting Vehicles.

atomic_text: 3. The jettison candidate identification, analysis, and approval process, including criteria intended to quantify and control additional orbital debris risk from any jettison candidate and the risk of collision a jettison candidate poses to the Destination and Visiting Vehicles.

source.verbatim_text: 4. Requirements that address the guidelines and practices in the U.S. Government Orbital Debris Mitigation Standard Practices (November 2019), the Inter-Agency Space Debris Coordination Committee (IADC) Space Debris Mitigation Guidelines (February 2025), and the Space Debris Mitigation Guidelines of the United Nations Committee on the Peaceful Uses of Outer Space, (2010).

source.verbatim_text: Quarterly nonconformance reporting includes all NCs currently being addressed with investigation and resolution boards (e.g., materials review board, failure review board, technical interchange meeting(s)) until their associated concerns, issues, and risks have been resolved or have been mitigated to the point of acceptance. source.verbatim_text: 10. Description/Use: Supply Chain Visibility (SCV) Reporting by Contractors to NASA is required to systematically build and maintain visibility into the supply chains for NASA mission programs/projects to enable pro-active Supply Chain Risk Management (SCRM) that supports situational awareness, planning, risk analysis and informed decision-making at NASA project, program, center and agency levels.

atomic_text: 10. Description/Use: Supply Chain Visibility (SCV) Reporting by Contractors to NASA is required to systematically build and maintain visibility into the supply chains for NASA mission programs/projects to enable pro-active Supply Chain Risk Management (SCRM) that supports situational awareness, planning, risk analysis and informed decision-making at NASA project, program, center and agency levels.

source.verbatim_text: The SCV Reporting application of the NASA Supply Chain Insight Central (SCIC) information and analysis services platform shall be used by the Contractor to securely fulfill SCV reporting requirements in accordance with instructions provided in this Data Requirements Description (DRD) and the SCV Reporting application. The reporting will be maintained within SCIC, which is part of a secure NASA / U.S. Government information system with access restricted to authorized users. SCIC is operated in accordance with NASA / U.S. Government requirements for the management of Controlled Unclassified Information (CUI).

atomic_text: The SCV Reporting application of the NASA Supply Chain Insight Central (SCIC) information and analysis services platform shall be used by the Contractor to securely fulfill SCV reporting requirements in accordance with instructions provided in this Data Requirements Description (DRD) and the SCV Reporting application.

source.verbatim_text: Initial Submission: Within thirty (30) days from contract award the Contractor shall use the publicly available SCV Reporting User Registration website (https://www.nasa.gov/visibility-scic/) to identify the primary individual and an alternative individual designated by the Contractor to perform the submission of the required SCV reporting. The Contractor shall use the SCV Reporting User Registration website to change or update information on the designated individuals within thirty (30) days of any updates to the prime or alternative SCV reporting roles. The SCV Reporting User Registration website provides instructions for the provisioning of secure access by the designated individuals to the SCV Reporting application. The Contractor shall submit an initial report via the SCV Reporting application within ninety (90) days after contract award.

source.verbatim_text: Contents: The Disposal Plan describes the Contractor's strategies, methods, and design considerations for the mitigation of the risk of an unplanned deorbit and for the planned disposal of the destination consistent with the objectives of the U.S. Government Orbital Debris Mitigation Standard Practices (November 2019), the Inter-Agency Space Debris Coordination Committee (IADC) Space Debris Mitigation Guidelines (February 2025), and the Space Debris Mitigation Guidelines of the United Nations Committee on the Peaceful Uses of Outer Space, (2010).

atomic_text: Contents: The Disposal Plan describes the Contractor's strategies, methods, and design considerations for the mitigation of the risk of an unplanned deorbit and for the planned disposal of the destination consistent with the objectives of the U.S. Government Orbital Debris Mitigation Standard Practices (November 2019), the Inter-Agency Space Debris Coordination Committee (IADC) Space Debris Mitigation Guidelines (February 2025), and the Space Debris Mitigation Guidelines of the United Nations Committee on the Peaceful Uses of Outer Space, (2010).

source.verbatim_text: Scope: The Operations and Training Plan documents the Contractor's approach to the activities, processes, standards, organizational responsibilities, interactions, and data exchanges required to execute operations. The plan covers all phases, including mission design, mission planning, operations product development, test and verification, training of crew and ground support team(s), and real-time execution for the CLDS. This plan ensures readiness of the operations products, ground support personnel, operations personnel, Mission Management Team (MMT), and the Crew for CLDS missions.

atomic_text: Scope: The Operations and Training Plan documents the Contractor's approach to the activities, processes, standards, organizational responsibilities, interactions, and data exchanges required to execute operations.

source.verbatim_text: Contents: The IDD includes allowable crew and cargo mass properties, mechanical interfaces (i.e., mount, ducting), electrical interface, data interface (including command capability), handling, any spacecraft specific constraint, and spacecraft environments. Environments includes, but is not limited to: quasi-static load, low frequency loads, random vibration loads, acoustic loads, thermal loads, pressure loads, shock, atmospheric gas concentration, allowable gas release, relative humidity, radiation, electromagnetic, and any other environment constraints deemed necessary. Environments shall be defined as the maximum environments that cargo may be exposed to during ground processing, launch, on-orbit, reentry, and recovery.

atomic_text: Environments shall be defined as the maximum environments that cargo may be exposed to during ground processing, launch, on-orbit, reentry, and recovery.

source.verbatim_text: The IDD shall provide an instrumentation plan including translation of data from flight instrumentation to the crew and cargo interface of interest. The plan shall include location and type of sensors, sampling rate, and downlink method and bandwidth for each mission phase. Launch and landing load factors and rotational accelerations shall be provided in the following reference frame:

atomic_text: The IDD shall provide an instrumentation plan including translation of data from flight instrumentation to the crew and cargo interface of interest.

source.verbatim_text: 1. The Integrated Maintenance and Logistic Support Plan (IMLSP) documents priorities, responsibilities, and develops cross-cutting initiatives for ensuring consistent implementation of programmatic logistics support and maximizing supportability, commonality, and affordability.

source.verbatim_text: a. Design Interface/Influence: Defines how the Contractor will work to impact system design to be supportable and sustainable; to include supportability management and interface with other disciplines, such as reliability, maintainability, fault management, and design engineering. Disposal planning (i.e. hazardous items, hazardous materials and fluids, and end of life of hardware items) is also considered.

source.verbatim_text: b. Sustaining Engineering: Defines considerations for anticipated support difficulties, continuous product improvement, design change assessment, service life extension, and compliance with GIDEP, obsolescence/diminishing manufacturing and material shortage (DMSMS) planning.

source.verbatim_text: c. Supply Support: Defines the supply support integration strategy including provisioning, predictive/prioritized sparing plan (taking into account the time required for procurement, build timing, life of the spares, and positioning (on-ground/on-orbit) based on sparing calculations), and catalog planning for nationally stock listed items for alignment and integration with Federal Logistics Information Systems.

source.verbatim_text: d. Maintenance Planning & Management: Defines the maintenance concept and planning considerations operations. source.verbatim_text: The Contractor shall define the process to evaluate and develop a strategic and tactical Destination traffic plan. This includes identifying visiting vehicle and element launch dates, traffic model groundrules, dependencies, constraints, crewmembers, manifest upmass and downmass requirements, key stakeholders, and sources of traffic planning information. The Contractor shall define the process to approve, publish, and publicly release the Destination traffic plan, including the necessary coordination between the contractor, NASA, and all other customers.

source.verbatim_text: The Crew Transportation Mission Integration and Operations Management Plan describes the Contractor's current processes, product delivery templates, and organizational interfaces necessary for the Contractor to execute all mission integration functions for a Crew Transportation System (CTS) mission to a destination. The document shall contain integrated text and graphics, as required, to describe and/or illustrate the various aspects of services provided, including process descriptions, schedule flows, facility and tool illustrations, and organizational hierarchies.

atomic_text: The document shall contain integrated text and graphics, as required, to describe and/or illustrate the various aspects of services provided, including process descriptions, schedule flows, facility and tool illustrations, and organizational hierarchies.

source.verbatim_text: The Mission Resource Allocation Document (MRAD) identifies the contractor's allocation of the transportation system resources and technical data requirements for integration of the crew and United States Government (USG) payload elements and their support hardware with the visiting vehicle pressurized and unpressurized (as warranted) volumes for each for Crew Transportation System (CTS) or Cargo Transportation System (CaTS) Missions to a Commercial Low Earth Orbit (LEO) Destination (CLD). The associated data will provide the required assessment to confirm compatibility with the spacecraft environments defined in the DRD-132 Vehicle IDD and compatibility with CLDP-REQ-1130 Requirements and Standards for Commercial Low-Earth Orbit Development Program and CLDP-STD-1102 Commercial Low Earth Orbit (LEO) Development Program (CLDP) Payload Interface Standard for Heritage Hardware . The report shall be generated, and the data updated based on progressive maturity of the mission definition and crew and cargo complement. The Contractor shall submit any changes or additions to NASA, in a timely manner, for the execution of the mission. The final MRAD data deliveries shall be coordinated with NASA. Referenced data is considered Type 3 for purposes of this document; however, if referenced data deliverables have a different data type than indicated herein, the referenced data type will prevail.

atomic_text: The report shall be generated, and the data updated based on progressive maturity of the mission definition and crew and cargo complement.

Should this occur, the Contractor Transportation Provider shall notify the Contractor that such delivery has been made to NASA. The Contractor is still responsible for the timeliness and delivery of the entire DRD to the Government.

source.verbatim_text: Scope: The Mission Resource Allocation Document (MRAD) will be used to demonstrate that the allocation of resources and technical data requirements needed for each CTS mission is in compliance with the Task Order (TO). The MRAD shall address specific technical and operational issues pertaining to each proposed cargo and payload item for Crew Visiting Vehicle (CVV). It will also contain recommendations for combining the proposed cargo items into an optimized crew/cargo configuration for CVV. Any technical or operational issues that could not be resolved shall be documented in the report with a recommended forward action plan. The MRAD shall be the source of accurate data pertaining to the mission-unique mass, volume, power, and other resources allocated to each crew member, cargo item, and its supporting hardware. The MRAD may point to previously delivered data or analysis sources/records as described in the data requirements descriptions (below) and confirm mission-specific variables are within baseline analysis space. Analysis shall be provided only if mission specific resources required to support flight specific manifest/parameters are NOT within verification analysis submitted for certification. If necessary, associated VCNs will be reopened, and data shall be delivered per DRD CLDC-102 Verification, Validation, and Certification Plan (VVCP), and DRD CLDC-106 Verification Closure Notice delivery.

atomic_text: The MRAD shall address specific technical and operational issues pertaining to each proposed cargo and payload item for Crew Visiting Vehicle (CVV).

source.verbatim_text: 7. Crew and pressurized payloads environmental assessment. For Crew, and USG payloads, the contractor shall perform mission specific analysis for all phases of the mission of the environment of the pressurized cabin environment. The MRAD may point to previously delivered VCNs and confirm mission-specific variables are within analysis space. Analysis shall be provided only if mission specific thermal/environment are not within verification analysis submitted for certification and as documented within the DRD CLDC-132 Vehicle IDD. Associated VCNs will be reopened, and data shall be delivered per DRD CLDC-102 Verification, Validation, and Certification Plan (VVCP), and DRD CLDC-106 Verification Closure Notice delivery.

source.verbatim_text: Order (TO). The MRAD shall address specific technical and operational issues pertaining to each proposed USG cargo or payload item. It will also contain recommendations for combining the proposed cargo items into an optimized cargo configuration for CaVV. Any technical or operational issues that could not be resolved shall be documented in the report with a recommended forward action plan. The MRAD shall be the source of accurate data pertaining to the mission-unique mass, volume, power, and other resources allocated to each crew member, cargo item, and its supporting hardware. The report shall be generated and the data updated based on progressive maturity of the mission definition and cargo complement. The Contractor shall submit any changes or additions to NASA, in a timely manner. The final MRAD data deliveries shall be coordinated with NASA. The MRAD may point to previously delivered data or analysis sources/records as described in the data requirements descriptions (below) and confirm mission-specific variables are within baseline analysis space. Analysis shall be provided only if mission specific resources required to support flight specific manifest/parameters are NOT within verification analysis submitted for certification. If necessary, associated VCNs will be reopened, and data shall be delivered per DRD CLDC-102 Verification, Validation, and Certification Plan (VVCP), and DRD CLDC-106 Verification Closure Notice delivery. Referenced data is considered Type 3 for purposes of this document; however, if referenced data deliverables have a different data type than indicated herein, the referenced data type will prevail.

source.verbatim_text: Contents: The contractor shall compile and deliver a Tactical Increment Execution Report comprised of the content defined below for a specific Increment. The TIER shall reflect the latest content at the time of DRD submittal with the understanding that the content will mature in the lead up to the Increment start. Whenever products are immature or not yet defined, the contractor shall include product templates for a typical Increment until defined. The Contractor may also make use of summary dashboards or metric reports to convey an integrated status or reflect product maturity trends over time in the lead up to the start of an Increment.

source.verbatim_text: The minimum resolution for the digital still imagery shall be no less than 4k x 6k resolution. Imagery downloaded from the camera shall be in a raw native camera format for maximum image resolution. Image cataloging data (Dublin Core metadata standard preferred) with include enough detail to support subsequent retrieval and shall be submitted for incorporation into the NASA Imagery Database. The preferred method of delivery is for the contractor to host the imagery within a secure and remotely accessible contractor repository, with NASA imagery clearly marked/tagged for quick sorting and a streamlined method for NASA to quickly export the NASA imagery.

source.verbatim_text: An Imagery Plan defines all imagery to be captured by the Contractor throughout the hardware build and life cycle including leading up to, conducting and completing a mission. The Imagery Plan will list all hardware to be imaged, the type of view (i.e. close-up, normal, wide view) and the integration stage of the hardware (before/after installation of shields, covers, labels or Multilayer Insulation (MLI)). The plan uniquely defines the Type 4 deliverable imagery to be delivered to the USG as defined below.

source.verbatim_text: 10. Description/Use: The Post Increment Report (PIR) summarizes the overall system performance of the Commercial LEO Destination (CLD) through the entire increment for a mission unique Task Order (TO). The PIR summarizes the CLD system health, consumables, critical spares, and any unplanned maintenance or future resupply required to restore full system functionality or maintain positive margins prior to the next USG increment. The PIR confirms the successful execution of all USG mission objectives and identifies any incomplete or deferred tasks. The Post Increment Report supports the NASA Contracting Officer's mission success determination.

atomic_text: The PIR summarizes the CLD system health, consumables, critical spares, and any unplanned maintenance or future resupply required to restore full system functionality or maintain positive margins prior to the next USG increment.

source.verbatim_text: The Unique Payload Interface Control Document (ICD) is designed to provide the requirements definition and interface details (including induced environmental) between the Commercial Low Earth Orbit (LEO) Destination System (CLDS) and the specified payload. An ICD will be required for each individual pressurized and unpressurized payload that has unique interfaces to CLDS not covered by the Payloads Interface Requirements Document (IRD).

atomic_text: An ICD will be required for each individual pressurized and unpressurized payload that has unique interfaces to CLDS not covered by the Payloads Interface Requirements Document (IRD).

source.section: [Unique Payload] to Cargo Visiting Vehicle (CaVV) Interface Control Document (ICD)
source.verbatim_text: Due to the potential transmission of competition sensitive data in this deliverable, the Contractor's Transportation Provider (CVV and CaVV) is allowed, with the Contractor's concurrence, to transmit this deliverable, or portions of this deliverable, directly to NASA electronically. Should this occur, the Contractor's Transportation Provider shall notify the Contractor that such delivery has been made to NASA. The Contractor is still responsible for the timeliness and delivery of the entire DRD to the Government.

atomic_text: Should this occur, the Contractor's Transportation Provider shall notify the Contractor that such delivery has been made to NASA.

source.verbatim_text: The DVVI plan shall define the data and products by which a crew or cargo VV will achieve technical integration with the Destination. The particular focus shall be on development of joint products and closure of verification requirements through Commercial LEO Destination (CLD) Contractor defined integration reviews, analyses, and tests. Further, the documentation shall identify the data the contractor is expected to provide to the crew and cargo VV Provider to be able to effectively develop their respective vehicles, mission support, and ground support systems and to be able to verify and validate the Destination to VV interface.

atomic_text: The DVVI plan shall define the data and products by which a crew or cargo VV will achieve technical integration with the Destination.
